summary: Tests make strong assumptions about the internal implementation of extensions/worktree.ts (command naming, exec-based ctx interface, body extraction format) without evidence from the actual source. The tests are likely non-executable against the real module and contain fragile coupling to an assumed API.

findings.title: Tests assume a specific ctx/exec API contract with no evidence from the actual source
findings.details: The entire test suite constructs a fake `ctx` object with methods like `ctx.exec()`, `ctx.pi.notify()`, `ctx.persistArtifact()`, etc., and assumes the PR command handler accepts this exact shape. There is zero evidence in the repository context or plan for what the actual `extensions/worktree.ts` module exports or what API contract it uses. The `getPrHandler` function assumes `worktreeExtension.commands` is a flat object with function values keyed by command name matching /pr/i. If the real module uses a different shape (e.g., a class, a registration pattern, different method signatures), every test will fail at the `getPrHandler` step before testing any behavior.
findings.fix: Read the actual `extensions/worktree.ts` file to determine its real export shape and API contract. Adapt the test harness to match the actual module interface rather than guessing.
evidence: const commands: Record<string, unknown> = (worktreeExtension as any).commands ?? worktreeExtension;

findings.title: extractPrBody helper assumes command is a single string containing --body with inline content
findings.details: The tests assume the PR command constructs a single string like `gh pr create --body '...'` and passes it to `ctx.exec()`. In practice the module might: use an array of arguments, use a temp file for the body (`--body-file`), use `ctx.exec` with separate args, or call `gh` via `spawnInteractive`. The regex-based `extractPrBody` helper would return empty string for any of these patterns, causing all body-content assertions to fail or pass vacuously.
findings.fix: After determining the actual implementation pattern from `extensions/worktree.ts`, adjust assertions to match how the body is actually provided to `gh pr create`.
findings.requirement: worktree-pr produces a PR description that references actual changed file paths from the diff

findings.title: Empty diff test has weak assertions that pass on almost any behavior
findings.details: The empty-diff test catches all exceptions and only fails if there's no graceful notice AND an exception was thrown. If the handler completes normally (even if it does nothing), the test passes. The post-completion assertion checks `prCall || hasNotice` which means any exec call containing 'gh pr create' (even from a default path) satisfies it. This doesn't meaningfully verify graceful handling.
findings.fix: Define more specific expected behavior for empty diffs: e.g., assert a specific warning message is emitted, or assert that no PR is created, or assert a specific error message. Pick one and assert it clearly.
findings.requirement: worktree-pr handles empty diff gracefully without crashing
evidence: assert.ok(prCall || hasNotice, "On empty diff, should either create a PR or emit a notice informing the user");

findings.title: Plan is too vague to derive testable behavioral requirements
findings.details: The plan's acceptance criteria are meta-requirements about documentation quality ('The feature behavior is documented in repo-grounded terms', 'The plan cites affected modules'). The actual behavioral requirement is simply 'improve PR descriptions to reference diff files', but there's no specification of the exact mechanism. The tests invent specific behavioral contracts (file paths in --body flag, minimum 50 char body, etc.) that may not match the implementation approach.
findings.fix: Acknowledge in findings that the plan lacks concrete behavioral specifications. At minimum, inspect the current `extensions/worktree.ts` source to understand the existing PR creation flow and write tests against that baseline.
findings.requirement: The feature behavior is documented in repo-grounded terms

findings.title: Missing major behavioral requirement
findings.details: No generated coverage entry clearly maps to: Review and update __tests__/extensions/currently-it-is-not-possible-for-me-to-work-easily-on-the-sa.plan.test.ts
findings.fix: Add an explicit behavioral test for this requirement or explain why it cannot be tested realistically.

findings.details: No generated coverage entry clearly maps to: (none)
findings.fix: Add an explicit behavioral test for this requirement or explain why it cannot be tested realistically.

findings.title: Missing major behavioral requirement
findings.details: No generated coverage entry clearly maps to: The feature behavior is documented in repo-grounded terms.
findings.fix: Add an explicit behavioral test for this requirement or explain why it cannot be tested realistically.
findings.requirement: The feature behavior is documented in repo-grounded terms.

findings.title: Missing major behavioral requirement
findings.details: No generated coverage entry clearly maps to: The plan cites affected modules or explicitly notes when no prior module exists.
findings.fix: Add an explicit behavioral test for this requirement or explain why it cannot be tested realistically.
findings.requirement: The plan cites affected modules or explicitly notes when no prior module exists.

strengths: Good coverage of the conceptual requirements: file path references, non-placeholder body, all-files coverage, empty diff handling, ordering of diff before PR creation
Well-structured test helper (makeCtx) for creating mock contexts
Clear assertion messages that would aid debugging if tests fail
Appropriate use of node:test runner matching repository conventions
